title: Somatic Therapy for Survivors of War and Combat
excerpt: Explore how somatic therapy helps war and combat survivors heal by addressing body-based trauma beyond talk therapy.
body:
Understanding Trauma in Survivors of War and Combat

Survivors of war and combat often carry wounds beyond visible scars. The intense experiences of violence, life threat, loss, and moral injury imprint deeply in the nervous system. Many have tried traditional talk therapy but still feel stuck—trapped by body memories of hypervigilance, flashbacks, chronic pain, or emotional numbness.

As Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, a pioneer in trauma research, explains, trauma is stored in the body and mind, not just the narrative memory. This is why somatic therapy—which focuses on body-based healing—can be a crucial path forward for survivors of war and combat.

Why Traditional Talk Therapy Often Falls Short

Talk therapy relies heavily on cognitive processing and verbalizing traumatic experiences. While valuable, it often doesn't reach the implicit, nonverbal memories encoded in the body. Combat trauma frequently involves intense activation of the autonomic nervous system—fight, flight, freeze responses—that become deeply ingrained physiological patterns.

Dr. Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing®, notes that trauma survivors can become trapped in these survival states, unable to complete the body's natural response to danger. This results in symptoms like chronic anxiety, panic attacks, and dissociation, which words alone might not resolve.

Somatic therapy offers tools to access and regulate these physiological states, facilitating healing at the root.

The Science Behind Somatic Therapy and Trauma

Stephen Porges' Polyvagal Theory revolutionized how we understand the nervous system's role in trauma. It highlights the vagus nerve’s role in regulating our fight, flight, and freeze responses, and how trauma can disrupt this regulation.

Somatic therapy interventions aim to restore the nervous system’s balance by tuning into bodily sensations and cues. Deb Dana, a leading clinician in applying Polyvagal Theory, emphasizes grounding survivors in safety through body awareness, which helps rebuild a sense of control and connection.

“Trauma is not what happens to you, but what happens inside you as a result of what happened to you.” – Gabor Maté

Key Somatic Therapy Modalities for War and Combat Survivors

Several somatic approaches have demonstrated effectiveness in working with combat trauma:

  • Somatic Experiencing® (SE): Developed by Peter Levine, SE helps clients renegotiate trauma by tracking body sensations and completing interrupted fight/flight responses.
  • Sensorimotor Psychotherapy: Created by Pat Ogden, this integrates body awareness with psychotherapy to process trauma held in posture, movement, and muscle tension.
  • Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR): Francine Shapiro’s method uses bilateral stimulation to reprocess traumatic memories, often with body-centered techniques.
  • Polyvagal-informed interventions: Based on Stephen Porges' research, these focus on enhancing vagal tone and nervous system regulation through breathwork, movement, and social engagement cues.

Practical Somatic Techniques for Self-Regulation

For survivors working on self-directed healing, somatic tools can foster nervous system regulation and reduce reactivity:

  • Tracking Sensations: Mindfully noticing where tension or numbness exists in the body without judgment, creating awareness.
  • Grounding Exercises: Connecting to the present moment via feet on the floor, feeling support from the chair, or breathing deeply into the belly.
  • Pendulation: Alternating focus between areas of tension and ease in the body to help the nervous system shift from hyperarousal to calm.
  • Resourcing: Activating positive body memories—like feeling safe or calm—to build resilience before approaching difficult sensations.
  • Movement and Stretching: Gentle somatic movements to discharge stuck energy and restore mobility and comfort.

Addressing Moral Injury through Somatic Healing

Many combat survivors grapple with moral injury—the deep distress from actions that violate their ethical beliefs. This type of trauma compounds PTSD and affects the body profoundly.

Gabor Maté highlights how moral injury involves shame and self-condemnation, which get lodged somatically. Somatic therapy helps survivors reconnect with their bodies compassionately, allowing them to process these feelings without retraumatization.

Integrating Somatic Therapy with Other Healing Modalities

Somatic therapy is not a standalone cure but an essential complement to other treatments. Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model can work synergistically with somatic approaches by accessing protective parts that hold trauma.

Combining somatic work with therapy that addresses cognitive, emotional, and relational aspects creates a comprehensive healing framework. This is especially vital for survivors of war and combat, whose trauma spans multiple domains.

Creating Safety and Trust in Somatic Therapy

Because trauma survivors often experience hypervigilance and mistrust, somatic therapy must prioritize creating a sense of safety. Therapists trained in trauma-informed somatic methods use pacing and attunement to help survivors feel seen and regulated before engaging with traumatic material.

Deb Dana’s work underscores the importance of co-regulation, where the therapist’s calm presence supports the client’s nervous system downregulation.

Conclusion: Why Somatic Therapy Matters for Survivors of War and Combat

Healing from combat trauma requires addressing the body’s role in storing and expressing trauma. Somatic therapy offers scientifically grounded tools to restore nervous system regulation, complete survival responses, and rebuild a sense of safety and agency.

For survivors who feel stuck despite talk therapy, somatic approaches open a new door—one that honors the body’s wisdom and capacity to heal. As Bessel van der Kolk reminds us, “The body keeps the score.” It’s time we listen with care.

faqs.q: What is somatic therapy and how does it help combat trauma?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy focuses on the body’s sensations and nervous system to release trauma stored physically. It helps survivors regulate their stress responses and complete survival reactions that remain stuck.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapy be done alongside traditional talk therapy?
faqs.a: Yes. Somatic therapy complements talk therapy by addressing nonverbal, body-based trauma that words alone can’t reach, providing a holistic healing approach.

faqs.q: Is somatic therapy suitable for self-directed healing?
faqs.a: Many somatic techniques like grounding and tracking sensations can be practiced safely at home, but working with a trained therapist is recommended for deeper trauma work.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy address moral injury in combat survivors?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy helps survivors reconnect with their bodies compassionately, allowing them to process shame and self-condemnation associated with moral injury in a safe, non-retraumatizing way.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma is stored in the body and the science behind somatic healing.
